user_story: As a subscriber, I want API GET responses to be cached per-tenant so that repeated dashboard or data reads are fast without hitting the database on every request.
expected_behaviour: The `@tenant_cache(ttl, tag)` decorator in `apps/common/cache.py` wraps DRF view `get()` methods. On a GET, it resolves `request.user.tenant`; if absent (anonymous or no-tenant user) the view runs uncached. It reads the current tag version from Redis (key `nbhd:tag:<tenant_id>:<tag>:v`), builds a cache key of `nbhd:view:<qualname>:<tenant_id>:<tag>:<tag_v>:<md5_sig>` where the sig covers path, sorted query params, and URL kwargs, then returns cached `(data, status)` wrapped in a fresh DRF `Response` with `X-Cache: HIT`. On a miss the view runs; if status 200, the result is stored at the configured TTL. Non-200 responses are not cached. If Redis is unavailable (`tag_v == 0`), the view runs uncached and the response gets `X-Cache: BYPASS`. Only GET requests are intercepted; other methods pass through immediately.
dependencies: Upstash Redis (via Django cache backend); falls back to LocMemCache in dev/tests
inventory_notes: Falls back to LocMemCache when Redis unavailable — invalidation is process-local only in that case. Tag version 0 means backend unhealthy; decorator bypasses rather than raising 500. Cache key includes tag version so atomic `INCR` on the tag key invalidates all reads for that tenant+tag without scanning Redis. Tag TTL is 30 days.

user_story: As a system operation, I want cache tags to be automatically invalidated when relevant models are mutated so that subscribers never see stale data after a write.
expected_behaviour: The `apps/common/cache_signals.py` module registers `post_save` and `post_delete` Django signal receivers for every data model that backs a cached API view. On any save or delete, `_tenant_id(instance)` resolves the tenant via `instance.tenant` or `instance.tenant_id`, then calls `bump_tags(tenant_id, tags)` which increments each affected tag version in Redis. Affected tag mappings: Workout/BodyWeightLog → `["fuel", "dashboard"]`; WorkoutPlan/PersonalRecord/RestingHeartRateLog/SleepLog/FuelGoal/FuelProfile → `["fuel"]`; JournalEntry/DailyNote → `["journal", "dashboard"]`; Document → `["journal", "sidebar"]`; Tenant → `["dashboard", "tenant"]`. Exceptions are caught and logged; signal handlers never raise into the request that triggered the save.
dependencies: Upstash Redis; apps.fuel, apps.journal, apps.tenants models
inventory_notes: Registered in `CommonConfig.ready()` so signals wire automatically on startup. If `_tenant_id` returns None (e.g. a top-level model with no tenant FK), the bump is silently skipped. Exception in bump is logged but not re-raised — a Redis failure during a write does not break the write request itself.

user_story: As an admin/operator, I want a quick smoke-test of the Redis cache backend so that I can confirm cache connectivity during health checks and deploys.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/cache.py:ping()` sets the key `nbhd:health:cache-ping` to `'ok'` with a 5-second TTL and immediately reads it back, returning `True` on match and `False` on any exception. Called by the `check_health` management command (`apps/orchestrator/management/commands/check_health.py`) which includes the result in its tabular and JSON output. Never raises; all exceptions are logged and return `False`.
inventory_notes: The 5-second TTL means a test key is never left in Redis longer than 5 s. The management command is also called by CI after deploy (`make health`).

user_story: As a subscriber using the web or mobile app, I want conditional GET support so that my client can avoid re-downloading unchanged responses and reduce bandwidth and latency.
expected_behaviour: `config/cache_middleware.ETagMiddleware` runs as the second outermost middleware (after `RequestTimingMiddleware`). For every 200 GET response with a non-streaming body it forces DRF rendering if not yet rendered, computes `ETag: "<md5-hex>"` over `response.content`, and writes it to the response. If the request carries `If-None-Match` equal to the computed ETag it returns HTTP 304 `HttpResponseNotModified` with `ETag`, `Cache-Control`, `Vary`, and `X-Cache` headers propagated. On a miss it sets `Cache-Control: private, max-age=10, stale-while-revalidate=60` (if not already set) and appends `Authorization` to `Vary` to prevent cross-tenant leakage by CDN or proxy. Non-200, non-GET, and streaming responses are passed through unchanged.
inventory_notes: Runs after `@tenant_cache` so it covers both cache HITs and MISSes identically. `X-Cache` header is propagated onto the 304 so `RequestTimingMiddleware` logging can distinguish HIT/MISS even on a 304 response. md5 is used only for caching (not security), hence `usedforsecurity=False`.

feature: Per-request timing and DB query count logging
user_story: As an admin/operator, I want every non-health request to emit structured PERF log lines so that I can trace latency regressions and N+1 query problems in Azure Container App logs.
expected_behaviour: `config/middleware.RequestTimingMiddleware` is the outermost middleware, wrapping the entire request lifecycle. It skips paths matching `/health`, `/static/`, `/favicon.ico`, and `/admin/jsi18n/`. For all other requests it uses `connection.execute_wrapper` to count DB queries while the downstream stack processes the request, then logs `PERF <method> <path> status=<N> total_ms=<N> db_queries=<N> cache=<HIT|MISS|BYPASS|->` at INFO level on the `nbhd.perf` logger. The `cache` field is taken from `response['X-Cache']` if present, otherwise `-`.
inventory_notes: Must be the first entry in MIDDLEWARE to capture total request time including all inner middleware. Health endpoint is excluded to avoid log noise from readiness probes.

user_story: As a subscriber using a Personal Access Token (PAT) to push sessions from external tools, I want rate limits applied per PAT so that a misbehaving integration cannot exhaust my account's resources.
expected_behaviour: `apps/tenants/throttling.py` defines `PATSessionIngestMinuteThrottle` (60/minute) and `PATSessionIngestDayThrottle` (5000/day) both extending `_PATScopedThrottle` which keys on `request.auth_pat.id`. Returns `None` (no throttle applied) for non-PAT-authenticated requests, so JWT/UI traffic is unaffected. DRF returns HTTP 429 `Too Many Requests` when the bucket is exhausted. `UserPATMintHourThrottle` (10/hour) is user-scoped (by `request.user.pk`) and guards the PAT minting endpoint against bulk token creation.
inventory_notes: Throttle state lives in the same Redis backend as the cache. Revoking one PAT isolates blast radius without affecting other tokens for the same user.

feature: Feature-specific rate throttles (chat, Siri, push, OAuth PKCE)
user_story: As a subscriber, I want per-feature rate limits enforced on AI, push-notification, and OAuth endpoints so that runaway clients cannot rack up unbounded model spend or APNs load.
expected_behaviour: `apps/tenants/throttling.py` defines user-scoped (`_UserScopedThrottle`) throttle classes applied to specific views: `ChatLocalTurnHourThrottle` (240/hour) for local turn record writes; `ChatContextHourThrottle` (120/hour) for the context digest endpoint; `SiriRespondMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) for the Tier-2 Siri fast-responder LLM call; `SiriStatusMinuteThrottle` (60/minute) for the Siri status snapshot (no-LLM); `PushTestMinuteThrottle` (10/minute) for the self-service APNs test-push; `AuthorizeMintMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) for the web→app PKCE begin endpoint. `ExchangeMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) is IP-based (`X-Forwarded-For`) because the PKCE exchange endpoint is `AllowAny`. All return HTTP 429 on exhaustion.
inventory_notes: `ExchangeMinuteThrottle` reads `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR` to honour the Azure Container Apps proxy header, matching the logic in `auth_views._client_ip`. Unauthenticated requests to user-scoped throttles return `None` (no throttle) rather than defaulting to IP throttling.

feature: Parameterised query view base class with internal-auth gate
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to POST structured queries to domain data endpoints so that the AI assistant can retrieve filtered, time-windowed data for any domain (finance, fuel, journal, insights) without writing raw SQL.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/query_view.BaseQueryView` is an `AllowAny` DRF `APIView` subclass. On `POST /api/v1/<domain>/runtime/<tenant_id>/query/` it first validates `X-NBHD-Internal-Key` + `X-NBHD-Tenant-Id` headers via `validate_internal_runtime_request`; mismatch or missing key returns 401 `{error: internal_auth_failed}`. On auth success it sets RLS context with `service_role=True`. It resolves the tenant or returns 404. It validates the request body against the subclass `query_model` Pydantic model; validation failure returns 400 `{error: validation_failed, detail: [...]}`. It resolves any `window` field via `resolve_window` in tenant tz. It calls `self.execute(query, tenant, window_resolved)` which subclasses implement; `QueryExecutionError` raised there becomes a structured error at the configured `status_code`. On success it returns a response envelope containing `data`, `meta.query_hash` (sha256 over canonical query tuple), `meta.computed_at`, `meta.tenant_tz`, `meta.window_resolved_to`, `meta.row_count`, `meta.has_more`, and `meta.schema_version`. Decimal/UUID/date values are coerced to strings/ISO via `jsonify`.
inventory_notes: Permission class is `AllowAny` — security is entirely via `X-NBHD-Internal-Key` validation, not Django auth. `has_more` is only set when `limit` is present and `row_count >= limit`. `query_hash` is reproducible for the same logical query at any real-clock time, enabling agent replay and test assertions. All exceptions in `execute()` that are not `QueryExecutionError` propagate upward as 500s.

feature: Deterministic time-window resolution for agent queries
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to specify time windows by semantic name (today, last_n_days, month_to_date, etc.) so that the AI assistant can query data for meaningful periods without hard-coding date arithmetic.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/windows.Window` is a Pydantic model accepted as part of domain query request bodies. `kind` is a literal enum with 14 values: `today`, `yesterday`, `tomorrow`, `all`, `last_n_days` (int value 1–730), `next_n_days` (1–730), `last_n_weeks` (1–104), `last_n_months` (1–24), `this_week`, `last_week`, `month_to_date`, `last_month`, `year_to_date`, `last_year`, `since` (date value), `between` ([from_date, to_date]). `_validate_value_per_kind` enforces that value-less kinds have `value=None` and value-bearing kinds have an in-range value; mismatches raise Pydantic `ValueError` surfaced as 400. `resolve_window(w, tz_name)` converts to a closed `(from_date, to_date)` tuple in the tenant's IANA timezone; `kind='all'` returns `None` so the caller skips the date filter. Week boundaries are ISO (Monday-anchored). `last_n_months` clamps to the last valid day of short months.
inventory_notes: `resolve_window` accepts an optional `now` parameter for testability; production callers omit it. `this_week`/`last_week` are ISO Monday-to-Sunday, not Sunday-to-Saturday. `between` validates `from_date <= to_date` at the Pydantic layer.

user_story: As a system component, I want a single authoritative function to resolve a tenant's IANA timezone so that date arithmetic is consistently user-local across all features.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/tenant_tz.py` exposes `tenant_tz_name(tenant) -> str` (IANA name or `'UTC'`), `tenant_tz(tenant) -> ZoneInfo`, `safe_zoneinfo(name) -> ZoneInfo` (UTC on missing/unknown), and `tenant_today(tenant) -> date` (today in tenant's local tz). All read `tenant.user.timezone`; if the field is absent, empty, or not a known IANA zone, the function falls back to UTC without raising. This module is import-free of `apps.tenants` (accepts `Any`) to prevent circular imports.
inventory_notes: Used by `BaseQueryView`, `resolve_window`, `resolve_relative_date`, cron scheduling, and any per-tenant date logic. `tenant_today` uses `django.utils.timezone.now()` so it is always UTC-aware before converting.

feature: Relative-date resolution for LLM tool inputs
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to translate date phrases like 'yesterday', 'Monday', or '3 days ago' into absolute dates in the tenant's timezone so that the AI assistant produces correct date-specific data writes without embedding UTC-based date arithmetic in its prompt.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_contracts.resolve_relative_date(tenant, phrase)` maps common natural-language date phrases to a concrete `date` in the tenant's local timezone. Recognised: `'today'`/`''`/`None` → today; `'yesterday'` → today−1; `'tomorrow'` → today+1; weekday names and 3-letter abbreviations (case-insensitive) → most recent past weekday (same-weekday → previous week); `'N days ago'`; `'in N days'`/`'N days'`; ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` strings. Returns `None` for unrecognised input — callers should prompt the user for clarification. `today_in_tenant_tz(tenant)` is a shortcut for the `'today'` case.
inventory_notes: When today is Monday, 'Monday' resolves to last Monday (not today) — this is intentional to avoid ambiguity in tool calls made early in the day. Returns `None` rather than raising on unknown inputs so callers can handle them gracefully.

user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want Pydantic validation failures in tool calls to be returned as machine-readable structured JSON so that the AI assistant can self-correct and retry with fixed field values.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_contracts.LLMValidationError` is a Pydantic model with fields `error='validation_failed'`, `message` (human-readable count + instruction to retry), and `details` (list of `{loc, msg, type}` dicts). `LLMValidationError.from_pydantic(exc)` converts a Pydantic `ValidationError` to this form. `as_tool_result()` returns `model_dump()`. Runtime views in fuel and other domains catch `ValidationError` and return this envelope as the HTTP response body (200 or 400) so the agent's tool-result message contains structured, self-correctable data rather than an opaque exception string.
inventory_notes: Error shape is documented as stable — agents are trained (via rules/_principles.md) to read the `details` array and retry. The `type` field carries Pydantic's machine error codes (`missing`, `value_error`, `literal_error`, etc.) which are more reliable for automated correction than English prose messages.

feature: Exercise registry normalisation and unit conversion
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want free-text exercise names to be normalised to canonical categories and metric types server-side so that mis-classifications by the LLM cannot corrupt workout data with wrong units.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_lookups.normalize_exercise(name)` resolves a free-text exercise name against a closed registry of ~110 canonical entries to `(category, default_metric)`. Resolution order: (1) `weighted <X>` prefix upgrades a bodyweight movement to `('strength', 'weighted_reps')`; (2) exact match; (3) longest substring match. Returns `None` if no match — callers fall back to the LLM-proposed value and should surface a warning. Categories: `strength`, `calisthenics`, `cardio`, `mobility`. Metrics: `weighted_reps`, `bodyweight_reps`, `hold_time`, `distance_time`, `blocks`. `convert_weight(value, from_unit, to_unit)` and `convert_distance(value, from_unit, to_unit)` provide exact unit conversion between kg/lbs and km/mi using fixed constants, raising `ValueError` on unsupported unit pairs.
inventory_notes: Longest-substring match means 'side plank with rotation' resolves to 'side plank'. The registry is the canonical source of truth — exercise names should be added here rather than expanding the LLM's context window. Float conversions use exact constants (`_LBS_PER_KG = 2.20462262185`) rather than rounded approximations.

feature: OpenRouter multi-model fallback client with health recording
user_story: As a system component making LLM calls for synthesis, extraction, or PII arbitration, I want OpenRouter calls to fall through an ordered list of model candidates so that a single model's unavailability does not silently break the feature.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/openrouter.chat_completion(models, messages, ...)` accepts an ordered list of OpenClaw-form model ids (e.g. `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`). `normalize_model_id` strips the `openrouter/` prefix before the HTTP call. For each candidate it POSTs to `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions` with `Authorization: Bearer <OPENROUTER_API_KEY>`. A 200 response is only considered usable if `choices[0].message.content` is non-empty (OpenRouter can return 200 with a top-level `error` on upstream failure). On usable response it calls `record_model_success(model_id)` and returns `(response_json, model_used)`. On failure (network error, non-200, or empty choices) it calls `record_model_failure(model_id, repr(exc))`, logs a warning, and tries the next candidate. If all candidates fail, re-raises the last exception. Raises `RuntimeError` immediately if `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is not configured.
inventory_notes: Health recording (`record_model_success`/`record_model_failure`) is fail-soft — exceptions in those calls are swallowed so model health recording never breaks an actual LLM call. `record_health=False` skips health recording for test scenarios. Timeout per attempt is 45s by default.

user_story: As a system component, I want to send push notifications to iOS devices via Apple Push Notification service so that subscribers are alerted when an AI turn completes or a proactive message is ready, even if the app is backgrounded.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/apns.send_push(tokens, title, body, ...)` sends an APNs HTTP/2 POST to `/3/device/<token>` for each device token. It requires all four `APNS_AUTH_KEY`, `APNS_KEY_ID`, `APNS_TEAM_ID`, `APNS_BUNDLE_ID` env vars set; otherwise returns `{skipped: 'not_configured'}`. Requires `httpx` with `h2` package; returns `{skipped: 'http2_unavailable'}` if HTTP/2 is absent. Provider JWT (`ES256`) is cached for 50 minutes and refreshed when within expiry. `collapse_id` is truncated to 64 UTF-8 bytes (Apple's limit). `content_available=True` adds `content-available: 1` for a hybrid alert+silent-wake push. APNs 410 or 400/`BadDeviceToken` responses append the token to `result['unregistered']` so callers can prune stale tokens. `sandbox` param selects the APNs host (`api.sandbox.push.apple.com` vs `api.push.apple.com`); defaults to `APNS_USE_SANDBOX` setting. Never raises — all paths return a result dict.
inventory_notes: Module-level JWT cache (`_jwt_cache`) means all in-process calls share one token. When `APNS_USE_SANDBOX` is False (production), tokens from Debug builds will be rejected by Apple — callers must pass `sandbox` based on the stored device environment. The `collapse_id` truncation operates on the byte boundary to avoid breaking multibyte UTF-8 characters and getting a 400.
